summary: This advice column addresses four reader questions covering math education policy, relationship negotiations around fidelity, supporting women in STEM fields, and dealing with difficult PhD advisors. Cathy O'Neil (as 'Aunt Pythia') brings her characteristic directness to each problem, emphasizing practical solutions over abstract principles. On algebra requirements, she questions rigid definitions and prioritizes statistical literacy for real-world health decisions. On relationships, she advocates for equity in negotiations rather than absolute rules. She encourages a mother to help her daughter find supportive math environments and urges a frustrated PhD student to actively seek a new advisor.
key_points: The definition of 'algebra' is contested, and what matters more than solving quadratic equations is the ability to critically evaluate statistical claims about health and research
Successful marriages don't follow universal rules but negotiate arrangements that work for both partners, with equity being essential to avoid resentment
Women entering math and CS often lack the proof-writing background their male peers gained from math camps, requiring supportive introductory courses
PhD students must advocate for themselves and shouldn't hesitate to change advisors when the relationship isn't working
money_quotes: I'm probably someone who would rather see people be able to read, understand, and dissect statistical statements about medical research than solve the quadratic equation from scratch
There is no absolute answer, there is only the negotiation you come up with with your partner. And the definition of 'it's working' is 'it's working for us.'

Most male math majors came in with lots of experience from high school math camps and math competitions in how to write proofs, but most women interested in math came in just interested and excited about math, but very little background in writing proofs
This is your life and your career, you have to advocate for yourself. Don't give up before you've tried everything.
key_insight: Whether in education, relationships, or career decisions, rigid rules matter less than pragmatic negotiations that account for real-world circumstances and power dynamics.
